div.p: Bélády's Anomaly occurs when increasing the number of page frames results in an increase in the number of page faults for certain access patterns. This is primarily a characteristic of non-stack-based replacement algorithms like FIFO.

Root Cause: FIFO does not respect the "Stack Property". In stack algorithms (like LRU), the set of pages in memory for $n$ frames is always a subset of the pages in memory for $n+1$ frames. FIFO violates this, leading to the anomaly where more memory equals worse performance.
Step 1-3: Warmup Frames: [1] [2] [3] Initial Page Faults: 3
Step 1-4: Warmup Frames: [1] [2] [3] [4] Initial Page Faults: 4
Step 1-5: Fill all Frames: [1] [2] [3] [4] [5] Initial Page Faults: 5
Step 6-12: Sequence Working Set fits entirely. All subsequent requests are hits.
